subject: Password expires in 10 minutes — reset now
body: Your network password will expire in 10 minutes. Reset immediately to avoid being locked out of clinical systems.
tell: Lookalike sender domain (hospital-secure.co, not realhospital.org). The link claims to go to the real reset page but actually points to verify-now.co. The 10-minute deadline is manufactured urgency — real IT never gives you 10 minutes.

subject: Your password expires in 3 days — reset here
body: As a reminder, your network password expires in 3 days. You can reset it any time before then from the employee portal.
tell: Correct internal domain, the link genuinely matches what it claims, and the timeline is reasonable — this is routine IT hygiene, not an attack.

subject: Update your direct deposit info
body: Payroll is migrating to a new banking partner. Please confirm your direct deposit details before Friday's payroll run.
tell: Typosquat domain — a numeral 1 replaces the letter l in "rea1hospital.org". Easy to miss at a glance. Anything asking you to re-enter banking details via email is worth extra scrutiny.

subject: Open enrollment ends Friday — action required
body: This is a reminder that open enrollment for benefits closes this Friday at 5pm. Log in to the employee portal to make or confirm your selections.
tell: Correct domain, link matches its destination, and the urgency is genuine — open enrollment deadlines are real and this is exactly how HR announces them.

subject: Quick favor — urgent
body: I'm stuck in a board meeting and can't talk. I need you to buy $500 in gift cards for a vendor appreciation gift and send me the codes. Handle this quietly, don't loop in anyone else, and don't call me right now.
tell: The CEO is supposedly emailing from a personal Gmail account, not the hospital domain. Stack that with urgency, secrecy ("don't call me"), and an unusual money request, and this is a textbook business email compromise attempt — no link or attachment needed to make it dangerous.

subject: Unusual sign-in detected — verify now
body: We detected an unusual sign-in to your account from a new device. If this wasn't you, verify your identity immediately to secure your account.
tell: The sender domain is misspelled — "microsftonline.com" is missing the "o" in Microsoft. Real Microsoft security alerts always come from microsoftonline.com or microsoft.com.

subject: Invoice 4471 past due
body: Attached is invoice 4471, which is now past due. Please open the attachment and process payment today to avoid a late fee.
tell: The hospital has no relationship with this vendor, and the attachment is a macro-enabled Word document (.docm) — a common way to smuggle in malicious code once macros are enabled.

subject: Invoice 882 for this month's supply order
body: Attached is invoice 882 for this month's medical supply order. Let us know if you have any questions about the line items.
tell: This is a vendor the hospital actually has an ongoing relationship with, and the attachment is a plain PDF — no macros, no executable content.

subject: Dr. Chen shared a file with you
body: Dr. Chen has shared "Patient_Intake_Q3.xlsx" with you. Sign in with your work email to view the document.
tell: The domain isn't the hospital's real document system — "sharepoint-files.net" is not an org domain. This is a generic credential-harvesting lure dressed up as a shared file notification.

subject: Sharing the schedule doc
body: Hi, here's the shift schedule doc we talked about at standup. Let me know if anything looks off.
tell: A real coworker, correct internal domain, and the link goes exactly where it says — this is an ordinary internal file share.

subject: New voicemail (0:42)
body: You have a new voicemail message. Open the attached file to listen.
tell: Voicemail notifications don't come as .html attachments — that's not how audio messages work. This is an external, non-org domain paired with a suspicious attachment type built to open a fake login page.

subject: STAT result ready for review
body: A STAT lab result for a patient under Dr. Osei's care is ready for review in the clinical system. Please review as soon as possible.
tell: Correct internal domain and this is exactly what the real clinical lab system sounds like. It's urgent because it's real — a patient is waiting on this result, and quarantining it delays real care.

subject: System maintenance tonight 2am — save your work
body: Scheduled maintenance will take clinical systems offline briefly starting at 2am tonight. Please save your work before then. Expected downtime is under 15 minutes.
tell: Correct domain, plausible content, and a normal heads-up notice. The urgent tone is just IT being considerate about downtime, not a manipulation tactic.

subject: Your assigned training is due
body: This is a reminder that your annual continuing education module is due by the end of the month. Log in to your LearnWell account to complete it.
tell: External domain, but it's a known continuing-education provider the hospital actually contracts with, and everything about the link and message matches what that relationship looks like.

subject: Following up on the schedule doc
body: Hey, following up on that schedule doc from earlier — can you take a look at the updated version and confirm your shift? Link below.
tell: A typosquat domain — "realhospita1.org" uses a numeral 1 in place of the letter l. This one specifically names a real coworker you've already seen this shift to borrow their trust. Late-shift fatigue is exactly when this kind of familiarity trick works.
